trundled it said ho could take me
there and back by sundown.
A thunderstorm delayed us, and
the sun went down while wo were
among the hills, two miles, at least,
from the city.
The coolie insisted upon my stop
ping at ono more ruin, in a grove
just oT tho road. It was so dark
that I could not have seen it, at tlio
best, and I told hira to hurry back
to the city. It is hard, however, to
convince a Chinese coolie of any

ruin.
It was almost dark in the grove,
raid as we approached the indistinct
misses of masonry they gave mo a
strong suggestion of all sorts of
hosts and goblins.
Suddenly the coolie began to run.
If ho had been going tho other way
shouldhave thought ho was fright
ened. Before I had time to think,
however, the barrow wag tipped
sharply to one side and I was floun
dering in the tangled undergrowth.
As I sprang to my feet every rock
raid shrub about me seemed alive.
Out of tho very ground men seem
ed to rise. I thought there were at

It would have been folly to strug

east entering, a vigorous protest,
when my arms were unceremoni
ously caught in a noose and securely
bound, my feet were tied, a cloth
was bound over my face, and I was
aid upon nry back in some sort of
a box.
It was all dono so quickly that
when it was over I could still feel

ailing out of tho wheelbarrow.
Not a word had been spoken, but
I know by tho motion that I must

was being carried somewhere,

The air was stifling. It was rank
with opium. Some ot tho fellows
must have been smoking there while

Between tho cloth over my face
and the opium fumes, which were
all the air J. could secure, I was suf
focating. I shouted, but it did no
ood; tho steady, silent swing kept

Evidently the fellows knew
what they were doing, but did not

The scene changed, and changed
again, and yet it did not change at
all. A thousand different "things
seemed going on about me; then
there was nothing at all going on

I shut my eyes for a short nap
When I opened them again I was
in bed in my gaudy little stateroom
Vpouthe mandarin junk, miles away
from Nankin, ou the great Blue

How came I there? Two men
had brought me down to the whar:
in a palanquin.
They said they came from an opi
urn den, and the officers of my ct?
cort, thinking I had yielded to

selves, paid tho fellows well for liav

My watch was gone; so were my
money, my pocket-knife, my hand

about me that could be of service
oven to a Chinaman had disappear
ed. They put me to sleep by burning
an opium rope in that closed palan
quin. It was the gentlest, m ost complete
highway robbery that I ever heard

THE PROVOCATION WAS GREAT.
An Old Connecticut Tiiiclior Thrushes a
jKcNoiilful Vdimx Man,
New York Sun.
When William St urges taught
school in the little red school houso
on the hill in the town of Wilton,
Conn., over forty years ago, ho was
considered a strict disciplinarian
among his pupils. Ono of their,
was Lorenzo Ohnsfcad, whom Mr.
St urges had occasion to thrash
soundly one term for a bit of devil
ry. Tho affair caused some talk
at the time, according to tho Wil-
tonites, but they all upheld Mr.
Sturges.
The other day, when Mr. Stur
ges, now a man approaching his
70th year, went to tho post-office to
get his daily mail, ho met a son of
his former pupil, named Harry
Olmstead, who is just 21 years old.
Young Olmstead stepped up to tho
old man and remarked:
"Mr. Sturges, many years ago
you gave my tauier, .Lorenzo by
name, who was one of your students,
a terrible thrashing. Do vou re
member it?"
The old schoolmaster replied in .
the affirmative, and added that he
deserved it.
" Well," said young Olmstead, " I
am going to lick you, my dear sir,
and pay up that old score with com
pound interest."
" If you think you can do it you
can begin now," said Mr. Sturges.
Some further words were ex
changed, and it was proposed that
they put up some money on tho re
sult Olmstead borrowed 50 cents
and placed it in the bauds of Louis
Verlin, which amount Mr. Sturges
promptly covered from an old sheep
skin pocket-book.
' A ring was formed and the old
man put up his fists. Young Olm-
stead, who had considerable confi
dence in himself becauso ho had
taken lessons in boxing from a Nor
walk sport, sailed in. Ho can not
tell to-day exactly how it happened.
Mr. Sturges warded off his blows,
seized him by his coat collar and
tiie seat of his trousers and shook
him until his teeth rattled. Then
he motioned for more room and be
gan to plow up tho ground with
young 01 mstead's nose. After get
ting quite a furrow he harrowed it
with the youngster's logs, and final
ly let him up. Tho young man was
now thoroughly mad, and, forget
ting the old score, with interest, ho
was going to pay on his father's ac
count, started in to pay off the pres
ent one. Again tho old man took
hold of him, but this time ho lost
his footing and came down on top
of his youthful antagonist. He
seized Olnistoad's neck between his
teeth and bit him severely. Then
he let go there and got hold of an
ear, and ho was chewing away on
that with great vigor when tho by
standers interfered and separated
the fighters.
Olmstead was a sorry sight. His
clothing was useless, even f it a corn
field scarecrow, and his face and
ear were bleeding freely. The old
man was excited, and his collar was
awry, with the old-fashioned stock
twisted aroundunder oue-ear. Not
a mark of tho affray was on hi in.
Claiming the stakes, which were
handed over to him, ho went home,
while Olmstead sought a secluded
spot to make repairs to his ward
robe. The affair created m uch talk
at Wilton, because Mr. Sturges is a
school visitor, town auditor, and a
pillar of the church. But the prov
oeution wns great.

Women's Societies
SUGGESTIONS.
By Miss C. L. Campbell.
"The supreme question of mis
sions Is how to multiply the number
of Christians who . . . will . . .
wield the force of intercessory prayer."
? John R. Mott.
? ' ? ? ? * * ? *
Twenty Questions On the Church's
Work.
Don't be shocked, but this is going
to be a sort of progressive party. To
carry it out, you will need seven
tables, and on each table a set of
questions concerning one part of
Church's work.
Table number one has questions
about your own church, asking names
of elders and deacons, power and
duties of the session, and all obliga
tions assumed by your congregation;
and so on and on.
Table number two will have twenty
questions about your Synodical and
Presbyterial and Congregational Home
Missions.
Other tables, questions on Home
Missions, Christian Educational and
Ministerial Relief, Publication and
Sunday-schools, the Woman's Aux
iliary and Poreigt Missions. (This
last tab'.e may neeti a twin.)
Seat your members, two, four or
six at each table, giving to each a
pencil and a card bearing the name
of subject in hand, e. g., "Foreign
Missions," and also having the num
bers 1 to 20 down the left side of the
card. The questions on each table
are written only on one large card or
paper.
On each table you will have placed
the literature giving all needed infor
mation ? your own Chruch hand
book, and It may be the Confession
of Faith will be needed, and all sorts
of things from the four committees.
(Begin to write them now; they like
it; it shows you are awake.)
Ask your pastor where to secure
information concerning Home Missions
near at hand; for Assembly's Home
Missions write Atlanta, also Atlanta
for the Woman's Auxiliary; and, of
course, you will write Nashville, Louis
ville and Richmond for material for
the other subjects.
When all are seated, announce the
time limit wrich you have deemed
wise; when this is reached, let the
players change to the table next in
order, until all have "gone to school"
at each table, if time permits; and
after this day of intensive education,
the members of your society will be
very intelligent workers, and ? yes ?
more intelligent pray-ers and givers.
Probably, now in the middle of the
summer, you cannot carry out this
program, but it is given early that
you may be collecting all sort of ma
terial from the four Executive Com
mittees, and other sources. Montreat
offers many opportunities.
This plan was originated and carried
through most effectively at Mizpah
church, Richmond, Va. Therefore, do
not be troubled. It is workable.
Be busy collecting material about
the duties, obligation, organization ?
the whole "raison d' etre" ? of every
part of the work; and on this page in
September the actual questions will be
given.
This is cordially commended as a
plan for that first fall meeting. No
body will have a lonesome time, or go
borne saying fche "had nothing to do
at that meeting."
A MESSAGE! TO THE WOMEN OF
VIRGINIA.
Have you seen a Litorature Box?
If you want one as a sample for your
society, you can have one just for
postage, five cents.
They are very simple, and easy to
make, but very useful.
The making of these is good hot
weather work: tho weather doesn't in
terfere with the box, and the box
makes you forget the weather.
Please write me soon.
Carrie Lee Campbell,
Synodieal Secretary Foreign Missions,
319 West Grace Street, Richmond,
Va.
THE MEN OF KOREA.
Ethel E. Kestler.
The men of Korea are divided
socially into many classes.. The first
is the Yangba class. The men of this
class have much money, usually sev
eral wives, and a host of servants. He
is the Korean aristocrat, and is looked
upon as somewhat of a lord and ex
pects to be treated as such.
Next is the so-called gentlemen
class, who wear a stiff black hat, long
white coat, and baggy white trousers.
This so-called gentleman is usually a
loafer, and while he loafs, his wife
is at home providing for him and the
household; and if she has not done
as he thought she should, she is
beaten or receives some horrible pun
ishment, and she takes It from him
as a dog would from its master, and
works all the harder that she might
plea&e him. And as he struts out of
the house with the white clothes rust
ling, that she had probably worked
' over until late in the night, her heart
swells with pride over him and she
takes great pride in announcing that
her husband does not have to work,
but is a loafer.
The next is the farmer class, who
work rather hard during the farming
season, preparing their rice fields by
first running the devils and evii spirits
out of the fields by means of a most
horrible set of musical instruments
that resemble a drum in appearance,
but which utters awful, doleful, deaf
ening sounds that seem to me any
thing might be glad to run a vay from.
This so-called music is kept up dur
ing the ploughing season, and the
workers march to It all day as they
are planting and weeding the rice.
The rice is planted in June and gath
ered or reaped in October. The barley
crop is then planted and reaped in
June. These two are the principal
crops of Korea.
The coolie class is usually a very
low class, but are willing to do any
kind of work. Some of them are train,
ed for some special work, such as the
chair coolie, who makes his living by
carrying the chair (the Korean lady's
phaeton; also used by some of the
wealthy class of men), and the chair
coolie who carries large loads on his
back. This class is the most filthy of
all the Koreans, and has very little
sense of respectability. Yet there are
classes below the coolie, called
butchers, who sell meat, and next are
the Buddhist priests, who are con
sidered very low.
The Korean man has from one to
several wives, as his financial condi
tion will permit, and this Is one of
the great obstacles in the mission
ary's work among the better, or rather
higher, class of Koreans. Many of the
wives have become Interested in Chris
tianity, but should they leave their
husband's home, they have no place
to go and no means of support. Yet
there are many who are bravely giving
up all for the Master and are facing
the hardships with a brave spirit of
resignation.
Korean men make excellent Chris
tians. Their zeal in telling tho story
of the Saviour's love, their interest in
Bible study, and their sacrifice in giv
ing, testifying to their faithfulness as
believers. The supreme need of all
classes of the men of Korea Is the
gospel of Jesus Christ.
TO CHRISTIAN WOMEN.
A message Is being sent to you by
one of your number. Will you not
read it carefully and prayerfully?
Were I a preacher, I do not know
whether I would select as my text
the verses, "Lord, open thou mine
eyes"; "What wilt thou have me to
do?" or "Wist yo not that I must bo
about my Father's business?" I want
to lay. upon your hearts those who are
out in the highways and hedges
around us, and whom our Lord has
commanded (not requested) us to go
out and bring in; compel them to
come in, and that means giving time
and effort and prayer.
Have we been obeying that com
mand? What does disobedience to
that command cost us? Oh, you
mothers, let me tell you something,
I was reading my Bible the other day,
with my heart heavy with the thought
of the little children in the slums of
our town: drowning in sin, and raising
helpless hands to us, and as I read
the words, "For if ye forgive not men
their trespasses, neither will your
Father which Is in heaven forgive your
trespasses," I seemed to see in black
print, "For if you stretch not out a
helping hand to the perishing children
of darkness around you, neither will
your Father stretch out a helping
hand to your children," and it seemed
to me that would be only the justice
of God. Do your children need help?
A lady said to me recently, "Talk
about the evil in the slums here, I
could tell you of things happening in
some of the best homes of our town
that would make your hair stand on
end." I do not know what she meant;
I did not ask her ? but I do know that
sin is the most contagious disease In
the world, and that if we let it go un
checked, uncared for, in the remotest
corner in our community, its poison
must spread even to our own; and
that is a just law of a just God. Oh,
women, who bear the name of Christ,
do you bear in your bodies the marks
of the Lord Jesus? What sacrifices
are you making for Him? The old,
old story of his sacrifices for us falls
to move us as It should. Thank God,
there are some who are faithful even
unto death. Have you not been deeply
stirred in reading the accounts of the
Armenian massacres? We know how
since the beginning of this Christian
nation In the fourth century, it has
stood the fires of persecution from
Turks and Arabs, and has grown
strong in the Lord through sacrifice.
But this present martyrdom has been
the greatest ever known In the world's
history; overshadowing the Spanish,
St. Bartholomew's, even Nero's at his
worst. As I read of the terrible suf
fering, the more terrible shame to
which their women submitted rather
than deny their faith, and so purchase
their release, my heart rebelled, and
I said, "Lord, if all things work to
gether for good to those who love thee,
how couldst thou let such things be
fall those who would not deny thee?"
And I waited, and after a time It
seemed to me I could see how they
with the glorious martyr crowns upon
their heads could even thank God if
by the unimaginable agony through
which they had passed for their Lord
and Master, they could shame hla luke
warm, careless, at-ease-ln Zlon fol
lowers the world over into rising
above their selfish lives and making
true sacrifices of time, talents, money,
thought, self, for the sake of him who
loved us and gave himself for us. And
what influence shall this example have
upon us? Do we know the meaning
of sacrifice? There is such crying need
of money in our foreign fields, our
home fields, our own communities. Do
we know what it is to do without a
new suit, a new hat, costly little furbe
lows of dress, the giving of a recep
tion, that souls may be clothed?
And as to personal effort, we say,
"Yes, I believe that if some Christian,
with God's love in her heart, were to
go to that poor sinner and lovingly
and tenderly plead with her, and show
her how to pray, her soul might be
saved, but I can't do it"; "I believe
if some Christian would take that
poor fallen woman who desires to turn
from a life of shame into her home,
and teach her in spiritual things as
well as in household affairs, she could
be saved, but I can't make the sacri
fice." "There is a child who if taken
right now into a Christian home and
trained will be saved, but I can't take
the trouble." "I ought to go at least
once a week into a home in the slums
and pray with and teach its ignorant
degraded inmates the way of life, but
I cannot make the sacrifice. I know
he who cannot lie has promised
enabling grace, and his presence with
me, but I cannot do it." Cannot?
Cannot, is it, or will not? God knows.
It la true there are some so situated
that they cannot, but are you sure you
are ono ef them? Tell God that, and
if you feel justified when you tell Him
so, then leave that alone and do what
ever work He gives you in your own
little corner. You give your money?
Yes, how much? Enough to make you
sacrifice, and so to bring you a bless
ing?
If in answer to prayer your eyes
are opened, you will not think of the
constant tiresome requests for money,
but of the blessed privilege of being
helpers together with God, and of sac
rificing self in His service. But you
will not feel service is a sacrifice, for
the joy of the Lord will abound in
your heart, and far more than repay
you.
Erlcuam.
THE PRAYER MEETING
TRUE USES OP MONEY.
Week Beginning July 22, 1917,
First Tim. 6:17-19.
Money has been said to be concen
trated life. It is the result of the
exercise of man's physical and mental
faculties. As such it ought to be used
in the same way in which we use all
of the faculties that God has giv?n
us. We should remember that we are
but stewards of God; and, as we sa*
last week, all that we have is Blmply
intrusted to us by God for use in His
service.
There are some things about money
which are not always clearly under
stood. It Is sometimes said that
"money is the root of all evil"; but
the Bible says, "The love of money
Is the root of all evil." There are
some people who seem to think that
all that they can be expected to do
Is to give a part of what they have
left after they have spent all they
wish to spend on themselves. There
are some people who satisfy them
selves about giving a small amount
to a good cause, by calling It the
